_comment: Curated catalog grounding the AI Pattern Picker. blog = /posts/<slug>; book = chapter slug in design-patterns-that-deliver (or null).

patterns.name: Factory Method
patterns.intent: Create objects without specifying the exact concrete class, delegating instantiation to subclasses or DI.
patterns.useWhen: You need to decouple object creation from usage and pick the concrete type at runtime (keyed DI, factory delegates, ActivatorUtilities, IHttpClientFactory).

patterns.name: Abstract Factory
patterns.intent: Create families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes.
patterns.useWhen: You need to produce groups of related objects that must be used together (e.g. cross-platform UI kits, a cloud provider's storage+queue).

patterns.name: Builder
patterns.intent: Construct complex objects step by step with a clean, readable API.
patterns.useWhen: An object has many optional parameters or needs flexible, controlled, multi-step construction (e.g. building URLs, requests, configs).

patterns.name: Prototype
patterns.intent: Create new objects by cloning existing ones.
patterns.useWhen: Creating an object is expensive and you can copy an existing instance instead.

patterns.name: Singleton
patterns.intent: Guarantee a single instance with global access.
patterns.useWhen: Exactly one instance must coordinate access to a shared resource (use sparingly; prefer DI lifetimes in modern .NET).

patterns.name: Adapter
patterns.intent: Make incompatible interfaces work together.
patterns.useWhen: You must integrate third-party or legacy code whose interface doesn't match yours (e.g. REST app talking to a SOAP gateway, unifying multiple cloud SDKs).

patterns.name: Bridge
patterns.intent: Split a large class into separate abstraction and implementation hierarchies that vary independently.
patterns.useWhen: You have a combinatorial explosion of subclasses across two dimensions (e.g. shape × renderer, subject × language).

patterns.name: Composite
patterns.intent: Treat individual objects and compositions of objects uniformly through a tree structure.
patterns.useWhen: You work with part-whole hierarchies and want clients to treat leaves and branches the same (e.g. file systems, org charts, UI trees).

patterns.name: Decorator
patterns.intent: Add behavior to objects dynamically by wrapping them, without changing their class.
patterns.useWhen: You need to add responsibilities at runtime and avoid a class explosion (e.g. coffee add-ons, resilient/retry HTTP clients, Scrutor decorators).

patterns.name: Facade
patterns.intent: Provide a simple unified interface over a complex subsystem.
patterns.useWhen: You want to hide complexity behind one easy entry point.

patterns.name: Flyweight
patterns.intent: Share common state between many objects to save memory.
patterns.useWhen: You need a huge number of similar objects and memory is a concern.

patterns.name: Proxy
patterns.intent: Provide a placeholder/surrogate to control access to another object.
patterns.useWhen: You need lazy loading, access control, caching, or logging around an object.

patterns.name: Chain of Responsibility & Pipelines
patterns.intent: Pass a request along a chain of focused handlers, each deciding whether to handle it or pass it on.
patterns.useWhen: Multiple handlers might process a request and you want to decouple sender from receiver (e.g. ASP.NET middleware, MediatR behaviors, approval/rules pipelines).

patterns.name: Command
patterns.intent: Turn a request into a standalone object, enabling queueing, logging, and undo/redo.
patterns.useWhen: You need to parameterize, queue, log, or undo operations.

patterns.name: Interpreter
patterns.intent: Define a grammar and interpret sentences in that language.
patterns.useWhen: You need to evaluate expressions in a simple custom language or DSL.

patterns.name: Iterator
patterns.intent: Traverse a collection without exposing its internal representation.
patterns.useWhen: You need a uniform way to walk different collections (in .NET, IEnumerable/yield).

patterns.name: Mediator
patterns.intent: Decouple components by having them communicate through a central mediator.
patterns.useWhen: Many components talk to each other directly and coupling is exploding (e.g. MediatR request/response, cross-cutting concerns, event aggregation).

patterns.name: Memento
patterns.intent: Capture and restore an object's state without violating encapsulation.
patterns.useWhen: You need snapshots for undo/rollback.

patterns.name: Observer
patterns.intent: Notify many dependents automatically when one object changes.
patterns.useWhen: One-to-many change notification (events, IObservable, channels, pub/sub).

patterns.name: State
patterns.intent: Let an object change its behavior when its internal state changes, making illegal transitions impossible.
patterns.useWhen: Behavior depends on state and you have sprawling conditionals or boolean soup on a status field (e.g. order/workflow state machines, guarded transitions, optimistic concurrency).

patterns.name: Strategy
patterns.intent: Define a family of interchangeable algorithms and swap them at runtime.
patterns.useWhen: You have if/else or switch choosing between algorithms (e.g. shipping/pricing/payment selection, Strategy + DI + Factory, config-driven selection).

patterns.name: Template Method
patterns.intent: Define the skeleton of an algorithm and let subclasses fill in steps.
patterns.useWhen: Several algorithms share the same steps but differ in details.

patterns.name: Visitor
patterns.intent: Add new operations to an object structure without changing the objects.
patterns.useWhen: You need to perform many unrelated operations across a stable object structure.

patterns.name: CQRS
patterns.intent: Separate read (query) and write (command) models for independent scaling and clarity.
patterns.useWhen: Reads and writes have very different shapes/scale and the complexity is justified.

patterns.name: Outbox
patterns.intent: Reliably publish messages by writing them to an outbox table in the same transaction as your data.
patterns.useWhen: You must guarantee a DB change and an event are published atomically (no lost/duplicate messages).

patterns.name: Saga (Orchestration)
patterns.intent: Coordinate a distributed transaction as a sequence of local steps with compensations.
patterns.useWhen: A business process spans multiple services and you need eventual consistency with rollback.

patterns.name: REPR (Request-Endpoint-Response)
patterns.intent: Organize APIs around endpoints, each owning its request and response.
patterns.useWhen: You want vertical, endpoint-focused API structure instead of fat controllers.

patterns.name: ServiceCollection Extension
patterns.intent: Group related DI registrations into clean extension methods.
patterns.useWhen: Your Program.cs/Startup is bloated with registrations you want to modularize.

patterns.name: Result (Errors as Values)
patterns.intent: Model expected failures as return values instead of throwing exceptions, composing steps railway-style.
patterns.useWhen: Failures are expected domain outcomes (validation, business rules, not-found, conflicts) and you want them explicit in the signature (e.g. railway-oriented Bind/Map/Match, ProblemDetails, ErrorOr/FluentResults).

patterns.name: Specification
patterns.intent: Encapsulate a business rule as a composable, testable object that also translates to SQL.
patterns.useWhen: The same business rules recur across queries and domain logic and your repository is drowning in near-duplicate query methods (e.g. composable And/Or/Not specs that translate to EF Core, Ardalis.Specification).
